Farn Polrrrcer. PRACTTcEs CouvrrssroN
428 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacramento, CA g5gl4-2329
(916) 322-s660 e Fax (9t6) 322-0886
March 27,2007
Mr. Thomas J. Haron
Chief Deputy County Counsel
San Diego Office of County Counsel
1600 Pacific Highway, Rm. 355
San Diego, Califomia 9210l-2469
RE
Your Request for Advice
Our FiIe No. 4-07-022
Dear Mr. Harron:
I Government Code sections g1000-91014_
18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations
Commission regulations appear attitle 2, sectiors
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of San Diego
County Planning Commissioner Michael Beck regarding the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").l Ogladvice is based on the facts
presented in your request and as supplemented by your telephone conversation with Fair
Political Practices commission staff on March l,iool. rhi rair political practices
Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby
(1975) I FPPC Ops. 71; Section 33il4.)
Also, please note that our advice is based solely on the provisions of the Act. We
therefore offler no opinion on the application, if any, oiother conflict-of-interest laws
such as cofitmon law conflict of interest or Government Code Section 1090.
QUESTION
May San Diego County Planning Commissioner Michael Beck participate in the
planning commission's consideration of a high-end housing project known u, n "
Bridges, considering his employment with the Endangered HtaUiiats League (..EHL,,),
a nonprofit public advocacy organzation?
CONCLUSION
Because a "nexus" exists between the purpose for which Mr. Beck receives
income from EHL and the planning commissitn,s decision on The Bridges,


File No. A-07-022
Page No. 2
Commissioner Beck may only p articipatein the commission,s consideration of The
Bridges if the decision would have no financial effect on the nonprofit organization EHL.
The facts provided statethat the planning commission's decision on The;;&", would
not affect EHL's budgeting, expenses lirrchair,g ernployee salaries o, u*o.rrirpent on
consultants), fundraising, or finances. on the uasis orttrese facts, plannin;
commissioner Beck is not precluded from participating in the decision on?h" Bridges.
FACTS
Christopher Neils and Debra Rosenthal, two attorneys representing Lennar
Development, the app-licant for a high-end_ trousing project known as ..ThJBridges,,, have
questioned whether Michaer Beck, a member ortte san Diego county Hanning
commission, can participate in the planning commission's consideration of their project
due to his employment with the EHL, a nonprofit, public advocacy organization which
opposes the project
You state that EHL constifutes a "source of income" to Mr. Beck pursuant to
section 87103(c), as he receives a salary fr9m the organization. EHL is pLely an
advocate for the preservation of endangered habitat itfr." than for the financial interests
of the members of its association. EHL's website is at www.ehleazue.ors. EHL is
funded primarily by grants from large qnti{es that zupport its conservation philosophy
such as the kvine Foundation and H-ewlett Foundation It does have a smali membership
that pays dues. Its.focus is primarily on region-wide issues such as comprehensive
general plans and theproposed northern uuttipte Species conservation plan but it does
take positions on projects that it believes wiil ;ffeciregional plans such as The Bridges.
Mr' Neils and Ms. Rosenthal allege that the planning commission decision on the
Bridges could aid EHL inachieving its goals. ftre nriages wants to develop some
sensitive coastal sage scrub lands wti"thay be key traultat for the california
Gn1lcatcher' They allege that this would have a "riasonably foreseeable financial effect,,
on EHL because: (1) EHL has threatened to litigate if the project is approved ; (2) EHL
is using paid consultants to assist it on this projel; (3) EHL wants to purchase the
property and a denial would reduce the cosq *a t+i iHt rro its work on piojects like
The Bridges to assist its fundraising efforts.
You contacted Dan Silver, the Executive Director of EHL and he advised you that
EHL had submitted extensive comments as part ofthe public review pro""r* urt had not
"threatened" litigation. He believes that the Escondido creek conservancy, a separate
and independent environmental advocacy group, would bring litigation. rt" utro thinks
that there maybe other community litigants.
Mr' Silver acknowledged that e{rJ. is usingpaid consultants on this project but
stated that it would do this anway and Mr. Beck'iposition on the planning commission
is irrelevant to this practice because this project is going to go to the goardlf Supervisors
for final decision anway. He stated thai rHt has-no pians to acquire the property but he


File No. A-07-022
Page No. 3
has heard that the Escondido Creek Conservan cy may. He stated that a specific project
like The Bridges would have no impact on EHL's fundraising. As noted^ubor", most of
its money comes from institutional grants from organizations that support it due to its
efficiency and effectiveness in advocating abroad,regional philosophy of habitat
conservation and not from an interest in a specific project. Mr. Silver stated that The
Bridges would have no impact on EHL,s funaing. - -
Mr. Beck hasreqlested that you seek written advice from the Commission to give
him guidance as to y-hether he may participate in the San Diego County planning
Commission's consideration of The Bridgis project grven tris emproyme"t *itf, the EHL.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits anypublic official from making, participating
in making, or otherwise using his or her officiai position to influence a goiJrnmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest. The Commissio" t * uaopt"a *
eight-step analysis for determining whether an official has a disqualifting conflict of
interest. (Regulation 1 S700(b).)
steps 1 & z- rs a pubric officiat making, participating in making or
influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of a local government agency, the San Diego County planning
Commission, Mr. Beck is a public official .rrd"r tire Act. (Sectiin g214g.) A public
9fficia-l "makes a govemmental decision" when the officiai, actingwithin ihe authority of
his or her position, votes on a matter, obligates or commits hi, ori", agencyto any
course of action, or enters into any contracfual agreement on behalf of tris or her agency.
(Section 87100; Regulation 18702.L) In participating in the San Diego county planning
Commission's consideration of The Bridgis project, CommissionerBeck would be
making govemmental decisions.
Step 3. Does the official have a potentially disqualifying economic interest?
A public official has a financial interest in a decision within the meaning of
Section 87103 "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material
financial ef,fect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a
me'lnber of his or her immediate family," or on *y of tt " offi"iul', etonomic interests,
described as follows:
o { business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000
or more (Section 87103(a); Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a
director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or hoids anyposition of management
(Section 871 03(d); Regulation I 8703.1 (b);


File No. A-07-022
Page No. 4
o Real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more
(Section 871 03(b); Regulation 187 A3.2);
o { source of income, includingpromised income, which aggregates to $500 or
more within 12 months priorto the decision (section ufii@i;nlguratio,
18703.3);
o { source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregateto $390 or more within 12
months prior to the decision (section gTro:(e);-Regulation wil.i);
o His or her personal finances, including those of his or her immediate family -- this
is the "personal financial effects"rule (Section 87103; Regulation lgTO;:;;., 
qlo
Mr. Beck's economic interests
Mr. Beck has an economic interest in EHL because he is employed by the
nonprofit otganization, and has received income from it aggregating $500 ormore within
12 months prior to,t: 1i^" the planning commission deciii-onJo., irr" Bd&;, wi1 be
made' (Section 87103(c).) Accordingly, planning "o--i.riorrer Beck shalinot make,
participate in making or use his official position ti influence a govemmental decision if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on EHL.
Step 4' Are the official's economic interests directly or indirectly involved in
the governmental decision?
Next we tum to the degree of involvement of the official's economic interests in
the decision' With respect to an economic interest in a source of income, such as
Mr. Beck's employer EHL, Regulation lg7M.l(a) states:
"(a) A per:9n, including business entities, sources of incomq and
sources of gifts, is directly involved in a decision before an ofiicial,s
agency when that person, either directly or by an agent:
*(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the dlcisioi will bemade by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or;
"(2) Is a named party in, or is the subjgct o{ theproceeding conceming
the decision before the official or the oin"ia,, ug*"y. A person is the
subject of a proceedin gif adecision invorves thJissuance, renewar,
approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract wittr, the subject petrson.',
' with respect to personal finances, "[a] reasonably foreseeable effect on a public official,s personal
finances is material if it is at reast $25b in any r2-morth p";;.; g;;htion r8705.5(a).) In this case,
however' there are no facts to suggest that the nlanning "o-*i*.iof J"'"isiol on The Bridges will have any
financial effect on thepersonar finances ofMr. Beck ihis immeaiaJamity.


File No. A-07-022
Page No. 5
If a business entity, source of income, or source of gift is not directly involved in a
governmental decision, materiality standards for an indireitly involved entiiy apply.
(Regulation I 870a. I @).)
Here the developer of The Bridges is the entitywho has requested that the San
Diego Planning Commission consider the project. EHL is not initiating the proceeding,
nor is it a named pafiy in, or the subject of, the planning commission,stnsfoeration of
The Bridges housing development. EHL is therefore tndlrectty involvedin the planning
commission's decisions concerning The Bridges development. (Regulation f AiO+.f 6Ul;
Because Mr. Beck's source of income is not directly involved inthe-governmental
decision we apply the materiality standards in Regulation 1g705.3@fbelow.
Step 5. Determining which materiality standards apply in deciding if there
will be a reasonably foreseeable material financial effecL
Regulation 18705.3(b)(2XA)-(F) sets forth different materiality standards for
sources of income that are nonprofit entities, depending on the financial size of the entity
involved. According to your facts, the annual gross r"".iptr of EHL are between
$100,000 and $1,000,000, so the following standard, uppiy,
"(2) sources of income which are non-profit entities, including
governmental entities. The effect of a decision is material as to a
nonprofit entity which is a source of income to the official if any of the
following applies: . . . .
"(E) For an entity whose gross annual receipts are more than $100,000
but_less than or equal to $1,000,000 the effect orm" decision will be any
of the following:
"(i) The decision will result in an increase or decrease of the entity,s
gros: annual receipts for a fiscal year in the amount of $50,000 o. *or".
"(ii) The decision will cause the entity to incur or avoid additional
expenses or to reduce or eliminate existing expenses for a fiscal year in the
amount of $12,500 or more.
"(iii) The decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of
the entity's assets or liabilities in the amount of $50,000 or more.,,
(Regulation I 8705.3 O) Q)@).)
Nexus Test
In addition !o the materiality standard abovg there is also a separate materiality
standard which applies in cases where there is a "nexus" between duties owed to a source
of income and to the official's public agency. The materiality threshold is
understandably much lower when a public official is paid by a private entity to
accomplish some action that is within the official'r p.rbli" decision-makinjauthority.
The "nexus test" is set out at Regulation 1g705.3(c):


File No. A-07-022
Page No. 6
"(c) Nexus. Any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on a person
who is a source of income to a public official is deemed material iittre
public official receives or is promised the income to achieve a goal or
purpose which would be achieved, defeated , aided, or hinderedly the
decision."
The rationale for the nexus test is that when an employee earns a salary to
accomplish a pu{pose thatmay be advanced by what he oishe does as u p"Ufi" official,
we presume that the employer is benefiting from the actions of the *proy"" in his or her
9ffi9ial capacity- (yarneil Advice Letter,No. A-00-r61.) Tpic alry, i..ol*,rr,, is found
in situations where the official is also ahigh-level employee with direct influence and
control over his or her employer's management or policydecisions. (MirerAdvice
Leffer, No. 4-03-147; Low Advice Letter,No. A-le_:Oi.;
Here, Michael Beck is the San Diego director for EHL; the executive director is
located in Los Angeles- Mr. Beck works on projects in the san Diego ^""itipa 
area and
works on a land trust project involving restoration of endangered rralitat. civen the
small size of the nonprofit EHL, and the fact that Mr. Beckis the organization,s San
Diego director, we assume he has influence over the nonprofit's pollcy a""irio*. ro tni.
case,, Mr. Beck receives salary from EHI, and atternpts to further th " irlu^iutron,s goals
ofhabitat conservation. In furtherance of that goal,^EHL has taken "po;t; opposing
the Bridges development. In the instance of the decision on The Bridges, EHL,s goals
overlap with Mr. Beck's decision-making authority as aplannirrg "orririrrioro.
Because Mr. Beck receives income to achieve a goalor purpor" that would be aided or
hindered by the planning commission decision, tI" rr"*o, test appries.
Step 6' Is it reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality standards
wiII be met?
Under the nexus test, we must examine whether the planning commission,s
decision on The Bridges will have "any reasonably foreseeable financial effect,, on EHL.
An ef,[ect upon economic interests is considered'ieasonably foreseeable,, if there is a
substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect need
not be certainto be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a mere
possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I Fppa Ops. 198.)
In this casg the facts you have provided from the executive director of EHL state
that the planning commission decisionbn The Bridges project would not affect EHL,s
finances T uny way- With respect to EHL's budget] tfre acts presented are that EHL is
primmily funded through grants from large entities such as the kvine Formdation and the
Hewlett Foundation that support its conservation philosophy, and also has a small dues
payrng-membership. According to the executive directoioibHt, f}r" nriagerproject
would have no effect on the funding for EHL, or on its fundraising or membership base.
The executive director stated that one specific project like The Bri-dges *o.rlJhu,r" ro
impact on EHL's fundraising


FileNo. A-07-022
Page No. 7
As far as litigation costs, the executive director advised you that EHL has not
"tlreatened" litigation in connection with The Bridges; he thinks that Escondido Creek
Conservancy, aseparate environmental advocacy doup and other community litigants
may file suit. As to any costs associated with *q"i.i"gproperty, the executive director
stated that EHL has no plans to acquire the property wf,; the aridges p*3".t would be
located.
With respect to consultants, the facts provided me that EHL has consultants on
hire on an ongoing basis, and annuallybudgeis a certain amount ofwork for their
consultants' The Bridges project is not a uig tuaget item and would not affect how much
EHL will spend on consultants. Accordingio EH:L's executive director, basically
somewhere in the neighborhood of $25,00b per year is budgeted by EHi for consultants
and EHL will spend that money on consultant wtrk on whartever projects it is doing. Its
budget for consultants would not go up oI down depending on whether The Bridges
project existed or not. EHL is primarily funded by grants and grant money must be spent
and accounted for to the grantor, so EHL will spenithe fixed amount on its various
projects' Further, the executive director notes tiat any efforts that EHL is undertaking on
The Bridges projectit would be doing anyway, irrespective of theplanning commission,s
upcoming decision, because the project is going to go to the Board of SupJnisors for
final decision anyway.
In sum, the executive director of EHL stated that the planning commission,s
decision on The Bridges project would not af,fect EHL's budget, financing, salaries paid
to employees, amounts paid to consultants, or in any way ater trre u-o*ith"
orgarization has to spend on things. He advised tfrat it is irrelevant to EHL as an
organization whether The Bridges project exists. under these facts, plannin!
commissioner Beck is not precluded from participating in the planning comlission
decision on The Bridges.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Hyla P. Wagner
Senior Counsel, Legal Division
